title: Chunk by meaning, not character count
linkedin_md:
# Chunk by meaning, not character count

Fixed-size chunking is one of those defaults that looks harmless until your retrieval system starts missing obvious answers.

You take documents, split every 500 or 1,000 tokens, add overlap, embed the chunks, and ship. It feels reasonable. The chunks fit the context window. The vector database accepts them. The eval set might even look fine at first.

Then production traffic arrives.

A user asks a question that depends on a definition in one paragraph, a constraint in the next, and an exception three lines later. Your retriever pulls the middle chunk, but not the chunk before it. The model sees half the logic and confidently answers with the wrong rule.

The issue was never the embedding model. It was the unit of meaning you gave it.

[YOUR STORY HERE - 2-3 paragraphs]

## Fixed-size chunks create artificial boundaries

Most documents are not organized in 800-token blocks.

They are organized around ideas: sections, paragraphs, tables, examples, code blocks, API references, procedures, arguments, exceptions, and caveats.

Fixed-size chunking ignores that structure. It slices through whatever happens to be at the boundary.

That means a single semantic unit can get split into multiple chunks:

- A policy and its exceptions
- A function signature and its parameter descriptions
- A troubleshooting symptom and the fix
- A table header and the rows that give it meaning
- A claim and the evidence that qualifies it

When this happens, retrieval becomes brittle. The right information exists in the index, but not in a form that matches the user’s query.

You did not lose the text. You lost the relationship between pieces of text.

## Overlap helps, but it is not a fix

The usual patch is overlap.

If 800-token chunks miss context, add 100 tokens of overlap. If that still fails, add 200.

Overlap can reduce boundary damage, but it also creates new problems.

First, it increases index size. A 20 percent overlap means roughly 20 percent more embedded text, more storage, and more candidates to search.

Second, it duplicates near-identical chunks. This can crowd retrieval results with redundant passages instead of bringing in complementary context.

Third, it still does not know where meaning starts or ends. A boundary can still cut through a concept, just slightly later.

Overlap is a bandage for a chunking strategy that does not understand the document.

## Recall dies quietly

Bad chunking often does not show up as an obvious failure.

The retriever returns plausible chunks. The model produces fluent answers. Logs look normal. There is no crash, no timeout, no empty result set.

But recall is lower than it should be.

The system retrieves something related, not the thing that answers the question. The model fills the gap. Users see answers that are close enough to sound credible and wrong enough to cause damage.

This is why retrieval evals need to measure whether the answer-supporting evidence was retrieved, not just whether any semantically similar text showed up.

A top-5 result containing the same topic is not enough. The retrieved chunk needs to contain the actual decision point.

## Chunk around semantic units

A better default is to chunk by meaning first, size second.

Start with the document’s natural structure:

- Headings and subheadings
- Paragraphs
- Lists
- Tables
- Code blocks
- FAQ entries
- Procedure steps
- Examples and explanations

Then enforce size limits after that.

For example, if a section is 300 tokens, keep it intact. If it is 1,800 tokens, split it by subsections or paragraphs. If a table is central to meaning, preserve the header with each row group. If a code example depends on the paragraph before it, keep them together.

The goal is simple: each chunk should be independently useful evidence.

A good chunk should answer at least one plausible user question without requiring the model to guess what came before or after.

## Add hierarchy, not just bigger chunks

The answer is not always “make chunks larger.”

Bigger chunks can improve recall because they preserve more context, but they can hurt precision. If every chunk is 2,000 tokens, the retriever may find the right document area but hand the model too much irrelevant text.

A better pattern is hierarchical retrieval.

Index smaller chunks for precise matching, but keep references to their parent sections. When a small chunk is retrieved, expand the context to include the surrounding section, sibling paragraphs, or the full semantic block.

This gives you the best of both:

- Small chunks for retrieval precision
- Larger semantic context for generation
- Less dependence on arbitrary overlap
- Better evidence continuity

In practice, this often means storing metadata like `document_id`, `section_id`, `heading_path`, `paragraph_index`, and `parent_chunk_id`.

The metadata matters as much as the text.

[YOUR STORY HERE - 2-3 paragraphs]

## Practical takeaways

If you are using fixed-size chunking today, do not rewrite everything blindly. Start with the failure cases.

Look at questions where the answer was present in the corpus but missed by retrieval. Inspect the retrieved chunks and the source document side by side. Ask whether the answer was split across a boundary, detached from its heading, separated from a table header, or missing an exception nearby.

Then adjust the chunker around those patterns.

A reasonable production setup often looks like this:

- Parse documents into structural blocks
- Preserve headings as metadata and optional inline context
- Keep paragraphs, list items, tables, and code blocks intact when possible
- Split oversized sections by semantic subunits
- Use chunk sizes as limits, not as the primary logic
- Retrieve small evidence chunks, then expand to parent context
- Evaluate retrieval against answer-supporting evidence

The important shift is mental.

You are not chopping text to fit an embedding model. You are designing evidence units for future questions.

Fixed-size chunking is attractive because it is simple, deterministic, and easy to implement. But it treats documents like raw byte streams when users ask questions about meaning.

That mismatch quietly kills recall.

If your LLM system depends on retrieval, chunking is not preprocessing plumbing. It is part of the model’s memory architecture.

---
**Tools I actually use**

- [Cursor](https://cursor.com) : where most of my agent-loop iteration happens
- [Beehiiv](https://www.beehiiv.com) : runs this newsletter's email list
- [Hostinger VPS](https://www.hostinger.com/vps-hosting) : the $12/mo box my agents live on
